title: Why skilled workers come to Germany and then leave again
titleZh: 为什么技术工人来到德国后又会再次离开
author: theanonymousone
relevanceReason: 命中 1 个 AI/科技关键词。
summaryRaw: "Anyone who wants to shape immigration successfully must also understand emigration," Laura Gossner, a researcher in migration and international labor studies with the Institute for Employment Research (IAB), said at a press conference in Berlin.

The IAB conducted an online survey of people aged 18 to 65 who had immigrated to Germany before April 2025 and then chose not to stay, asking them what factors influenced their decision to leave the country again.
The researchers found that emigration is not driven by any single factor. Family-related reasons, in particular, play a major role. Experiences of discrimination were also cited.
Many of the contributing factors, such as bureaucracy, housing and language acquisition, can be influenced by public policy.
Labor shortage forces Germany to accept more qualificationsTo view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that supports HTML5 video
summaryZh: "Anyone who wants to shape immigration successfully must also understand emigration," Laura Gossner, a researcher in migration and international labor studies with the Institute for Employment Research (IAB), said at a press conference in Berlin.

The IAB conducted an online survey of people aged 18 to 65 who had immigrated to Germany before April 2025 and then chose not to stay, asking them what factors influenced their decision to leave the country again.

comments.author: ikut3hva
comments.textRawHtml:
Hi everyone

I don’t usually comment on topics like this because there are so many biases and different perspectives involved. In the end, I believe only the person who has actually gone through the experience can truly understand it; otherwise, it often becomes just another judgment.

We are an ASEAN family earning more than €200k gross annually (sorry for mentioning the TC, but there is a reason for it—please keep reading before judging). We have lived here for more than six years, and you know what? I still haven’t obtained either permanent residence or German citizenship simply because I don’t have a B1 certificate. So first things first: regardless of how much you contribute to the country, German is a must today if you want to obtain residency and stabilize your life here.

I was honestly devastated when the officer told me that I was not eligible for permanent residence. That was also the moment when I started to feel that maybe I don’t actually need permanent residence in this country after all.

Story 2: In an international working environment, German may not matter much at the IC level. But I’ve seen countless situations where Germans exchange a glance with each other, and suddenly the final decision is not what was agreed upon in the meeting. Over time, I’ve learned that there are many unwritten rules behind the scenes, and when you speak their language, you start to understand them.

One bright thing is that maybe we’re still lucky. We bought our first home without fully understanding the laws, the government system, or the tax rules. We simply worked hard and played the game in a way that we believed would be sustainable in the long run. Whatever happens, we know there are still many other places we could go.

Our children speak German natively, but they are also willing to go the extra mile to speak our mother tongue at home.

If you ask me for one piece of advice for immigrants and emigrants in Germany, I’d say: life is short—play naked!
comments.textZhHtml:
大家好

我通常不会评论这样的话题，因为其中涉及很多偏见和不同的观点。最后，我相信只有真正经历过的人才能真正理解；否则，它往往会成为另一个判断。

我们是一个年收入超过20万欧元的东盟家庭（很抱歉提到TC，但这是有原因的——请在判断之前继续阅读）。我们在这里住了六年多了，你知道吗？我仍然没有获得永久居留权或德国公民身份，只是因为我没有 B1 证书。因此，首先要做的事情是：无论您为国家做出了多少贡献，如果您想获得居留权并在这里稳定生活，德语都是必须的。

当官员告诉我我没有资格获得永久居留权时，我真的感到很沮丧。也是在那一刻，我开始觉得也许我实际上并不需要在这个国家永久居留。

故事 2：在国际工作环境中，德语在 IC 层面上可能并不重要。但我见过无数次这样的情况：德国人互相交换了一个眼神，突然间最终的决定并不是会议上达成的一致。随着时间的推移，我了解到幕后有许多不成文的规则，当你说他们的语言时，你开始理解它们。

一件好事是，也许我们仍然很幸运。我们在没有完全了解法律、政府制度或税收规则的情况下购买了第一套房子。我们只是努力工作，并以我们认为从长远来看可持续的方式玩游戏。无论发生什么，我们知道我们还有很多其他地方可以去。

我们的孩子的母语是德语，但他们也愿意在家里多花点力气说我们的母语。

如果你问我对德国的移民和移民有什么建议，我会说：生命短暂——赤身裸体！

children.author: dgs_sgd
children.textRawHtml:
I appreciate your perspective, but I was curious what B1 proficiency actually entails and this is what I found [1]:

- understand the main points of clear standard speech on familiar topics such as work, school, or leisure - manage most situations that occur while traveling in German-speaking areas - produce simple, connected text on familiar subjects - describe experiences, events, dreams, hopes, and ambitions, and briefly explain your opinions or plans

That seems like a reasonable standard of native language proficiency to ask of people who want to make the county with said language their permanent home.

[1] https://www.sprachenatelier-berlin.de/en/topic/3736.german-p...
children.textZhHtml:
我很欣赏你的观点，但我很好奇 B1 熟练程度实际上意味着什么，这就是我发现的 [1]：

- 理解关于工作、学校或休闲等熟悉主题的清晰标准演讲的要点 - 管理在德语地区旅行时发生的大多数情况 - 就熟悉的主题生成简单、相互关联的文本 - 描述经历、事件、梦想、希望和抱负，并简要解释您的意见或计划

对于那些想要将使用该语言的县作为永久家园的人来说，这似乎是一个合理的母语熟练程度标准。

[1] https://www.sprachenatelier-berlin.de/en/topic/3736.german-p...

children.author: TheOtherHobbes
children.textRawHtml:
B1 is a completely fair minimum standard. It's normal for many countries to expect residents to have basic conversational adequacy.

It's also the kind of requirement that's made explicit on government information about residency. So it shouldn't have been a surprise.
children.textZhHtml:
B1 是一个完全公平的最低标准。对于许多国家来说，期望居民具备基本的会话能力是很正常的。

这也是政府对居住信息提出的明确要求。所以这并不奇怪。

children.author: DiskoHexyl
children.textRawHtml:
It is completely fair to expect at least B1 from potential citizens and permanent residents.

What the article is covering are the skilled workers, and for them B1 isn't nearly enough to function in any profesional capacity. I'd put B2 as a bare minimum for that. So if it's impossible for someone to find employment in the language they already know (for skilled workers it's usually English), they are understandably not eager to stay.

For those who are quick to judge, try mastering a foreign language (and German is noticeably more difficult than English, French or Spanish) in another country where you don't have any support system yet, and with likely some suboptimal job (as you need this foreign language to find a decent one). Getting to B2 takes about 750 class hours (not including homework) for an average English speaker. That's an enormous investment (of both time and money). One will have to sacrifice entertainment, socializing, hobbies, sleep, and work-related educational opportunities for several years in a row, depending on abilities (and the language).

And just a reminder- most people tend to overestimate how well they know a foreign language. Telling you know [insert your flavor of Chinese] and actually passing an exam are two different things.

Again, I am not saying it is unfair for country officials to expect whatever language level they want. It is their country and their rules. As far as I understand nobody's talking about giving away passports.

It's just when it's relatively more difficult for skilled immigrants to find employment and interact with said officials without high level (at least B2) German, some of these immigrants will chose in favor of the countries- with easier/more widely used languages and/or more jobs in English or just lower taxes and higher pay.

If a country's government wants to attract these kinds of immigrants, some of these variables will have to change
children.textZhHtml:
对潜在公民和永久居民的期望至少达到 B1 是完全公平的。

本文涵盖的是技术工人，对他们来说，B1 不足以发挥任何专业能力。我认为 B2 是最低限度。因此，如果某人不可能用他们已经掌握的语言找到工作（对于技术工人来说通常是英语），他们就不急于留下来，这是可以理解的。

对于那些快速判断的人来说，尝试在另一个国家掌握一门外语（德语明显比英语、法语或西班牙语更难），在那里你还没有任何支持系统，并且可能有一些次优的工作（因为你需要这门外语才能找到一份体面的工作）。 对于一个普通的说英语的人来说，达到 B2 水平大约需要 750 个课时（不包括家庭作业）。这是一项巨大的投资（时间和金钱）。根据能力（和语言），一个人将不得不连续几年牺牲娱乐、社交、爱好、睡眠和与工作相关的教育机会。

提醒一下，大多数人往往会高估自己对一门外语的了解程度。告诉你知道[插入你的中文风格]和实际通过考试是两件不同的事情。

再说一遍，我并不是说国家官员期望他们想要的语言水平是不公平的。这是他们的国家和他们的规则。据我所知，没有人在谈论放弃护照。

只是当技术移民找工作并与没有高水平（至少 B2）德语的上述官员互动相对更加困难时，其中一些移民会选择有利于语言更容易/更广泛使用和/或更多英语工作的国家，或者只是更低的税收和更高的工资。

如果一个国家的政府想要吸引这类移民，其中一些变量必须改变

children.author: dgs_sgd
children.textRawHtml: Like, I imagine if I moved to a country and couldn’t do the B1 things in their language, I would have daily obstacles doing basic life things.
children.textZhHtml: 就像，我想象如果我搬到一个国家并且无法用他们的语言做 B1 的事情，我每天做基本生活的事情都会遇到障碍。

children.author: 14u2c
children.textRawHtml:
Not in most of Western and Central Europe. Everyone speaks great English.

It actually can be a problem because most people switch to English as soon as they realize your are not a native speaker, which can make learning harder.
children.textZhHtml:
西欧和中欧的大部分地区没有。每个人的英语都说得很好。

这实际上可能是一个问题，因为大多数人一意识到您不是母语者就改用英语，这会让学习变得更加困难。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
This was my experience. I lived for almost four years in Berlin, and was trying to learn German (I got to A2, which isn't much). But actually getting experience/immersion in German is difficult in Berlin - everyone under the age of 50 speaks good English and switched to that as soon as my first badly-conjugated sentence left my mouth.

I worked for two German companies while I was there, and the entire tech team in both companies did everything in English. This is normal there. In one of them, the (German) CEO gave a townhall speech to the entire company (~1000 employees) in English.

So yes, it's very understandable for the German government to say "you must be at least B1 to live here" but that doesn't recognise the fact that it is perfectly possible to actually live and work in Berlin with no German at all.

It's different in other German cities, apparently. If we'd moved to Munich or Cologne I would have had more pressure to learn German, and also more exposure to it and more immersion which would have helped learn it.
children.textZhHtml:
这是我的经历。我在柏林住了将近四年，一直在努力学习德语（我达到了 A2，这并不多）。但实际上在柏林获得德语的经验/沉浸式学习是很困难的 - 每个 50 岁以下的人都会说一口流利的英语，当我说出第一句话时，他们就转向英语。

我在那里时曾为两家德国公司工作过，两家公司的整个技术团队都用英语做所有事情。这在那里很正常。在其中一篇文章中，（德国）首席执行官用英语向整个公司（约 1000 名员工）发表了市政厅演讲。

所以，是的，德国政府说“你必须至少达到 B1 才能住在这里”是可以理解的，但这并没有认识到一个事实，即完全没有德语也完全有可能在柏林生活和工作。

显然，这在其他德国城市是不同的。如果我们搬到慕尼黑或科隆，我会面临更大的学习德语的压力，也会有更多的接触德语和更多的沉浸感，这将有助于学习德语。

children.author: thayne
children.textRawHtml:
Even when I vacationed in Bayern (Bavaria), a lot of people switched to English when they realized that my German wasn't that good. Which was frustrating, because I wanted to practice speaking with native speakers.

Although, there were also people who only spoke German.
children.textZhHtml:
即使当我在拜仁（巴伐利亚）度假时，很多人在意识到我的德语不太好时也改用英语。这很令人沮丧，因为我想练习与母语人士交谈。

尽管如此，也有人只说德语。

children.author: doikor
children.textRawHtml: If you want to learn you just pretend that you don’t speak English and keep on speaking German. They will switch back fast.
children.textZhHtml: 如果你想学习，你就假装你不会说英语，然后继续说德语。他们会很快切换回来。

children.author: flakeoil
children.textRawHtml:
This is the right way. Speak the language you want. Even if they reply to you in English, and they know you can speak English, continue in German. They will understand. They switch to English because they want to be helpful. If you continue in German they understand you prefer it that way.

Another thing is that once you start talking with a person (who you meet on a regular basis) in one language, it is hard to switch to another language at a later date. So if you start with English, it is hard to go back to German. But if you start with German, even if it is less fluent for you, it will come natural that with that person you speak in German.
children.textZhHtml:
这是正确的方法。说您想要的语言。即使他们用英语回复您，并且他们知道您会说英语，也请继续用德语。他们会理解的。他们改用英语是因为他们想提供帮助。如果您继续使用德语，他们会理解您更喜欢这种方式。

另一件事是，一旦您开始用一种语言与某人（您经常遇到的人）交谈，以后就很难切换到另一种语言。所以如果你从英语开始，就很难再回到德语。但如果你从德语开始，即使你不太流利，你也会很自然地和那个人说德语。

children.author: throwaway2037
children.textRawHtml:
Did you ask if you can move to a different city and work remotely? Even if only 1-2 years, it might really help to boost your German. Moving to Dresden, Hanover, or Hamburg would help. (German HNers can probably recommend a bunch of small cities 1-2 hours away by train/car from Berlin that will use much more German language!) You could come to the office once a week.

Another tip: Always start the conversation in German. If the person switches to English, ask them politely to speak German, and explain that you are studying German. My parents did similar when they moved to the Netherlands 50 years ago. The local shopkeepers were eager to practice English with my parents, but my parents were also eager to practice their Dutch.
children.textZhHtml:
您是否询问过是否可以搬到其他城市并远程工作？ 即使只有 1-2 年，也可能确实有助于提高您的德语。 搬到德累斯顿、汉诺威或汉堡会有帮助。 （德国人可能会推荐一些距离柏林 1-2 小时火车/汽车车程的小城市，这些城市会使用更多德语！）您可以每周来办公室一次。

另一个提示：始终用德语开始对话。 如果对方改用英语，请礼貌地请他们说德语，并解释说您正在学习德语。 50 年前，我的父母搬到荷兰时也做了类似的事情。 当地的店主很想和我的父母一起练习英语，但我的父母也很想练习荷兰语。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
That's really good advice, but I found it hard to put into practice because my German was so bad. And German isn't a language you can just hammer out a badly-formed sentence in; word position is less important than conjugation, so if you get the conjugation wrong it makes no sense at all to the listener. I did try this a few times, but everyone lost patience with the painful butchering of their language when they realised their English was a lot better than my German.

I would have moved city, but a: COVID b: my wife also had a job there c: I really loved Berlin and really enjoyed living there
children.textZhHtml:
这确实是一个很好的建议，但我发现很难付诸实践，因为我的德语太糟糕了。德语不是一种你可以敲出一个结构错误的句子的语言；词位不如词形变化重要，所以如果词形变化错误，听者就毫无意义。我确实尝试过几次，但当每个人意识到他们的英语比我的德语好得多时，他们都对语言的痛苦屠宰失去了耐心。

我本来会搬到城市，但是a：新冠病毒b：我妻子在那里也有一份工作c：我真的很喜欢柏林，真的很喜欢在那里生活

children.author: throwaway2037
children.textRawHtml: I like the effort! It is more that half of the battle when learning a new language as an adult. What really helped me: Create your own custom Anki deck. Literally, start from zero. Each time you try-and-fail (or think of something that you don't know how to say), write a note to yourself in English/broken German with the sentence/idea that failed. At home, use a translate tool (LLM/Google/whatever) to build the correct German sentence. Next, add it to your Anki deck. Personally, I only add full sentences, never just vocab, because it adds more natural context. Only add real stuff that you will use. On the first day, you have an Anki deck with one card. If you are really motivated, try to add one card per day, but just 1-2 cards per week will quickly improve the deck. Since the deck is so damn small, you can blow through it in five minutes while riding the S-Bahn/U-Bahn trains. And, every single sentence is useful to you. Bonus: Ask a native German speaker about some of your Anki deck sentences. They will probably love to talk to you about the German language because you will be showing your commitment.
children.textZhHtml: 我喜欢这种努力！ 作为一个成年人，学习一门新语言就已经成功了一半。 真正帮助我的是：创建您的自己的自定义 Anki 牌组。 顾名思义，就是从零开始。 每次你尝试失败（或者想到一些你不知道怎么说的事情）时，用英语/蹩脚的德语给自己写一张纸条，写上失败的句子/想法。 在家里，使用翻译工具（法学硕士/谷歌/其他）来构建正确的德语句子。 接下来，将其添加到您的 Anki 套牌中。 就我个人而言，我只添加完整的句子，而不仅仅是词汇，因为它添加了更自然的上下文。 仅添加您将使用的真实内容。 第一天，您将拥有一套包含一张卡片的 Anki 套牌。 如果您确实有动力，请尝试每天添加一张卡，但每周只需添加 1-2 张卡即可快速改善牌组。 由于甲板实在是太小了，乘坐 S-Bahn/U-Bahn 火车只需五分钟就可以穿过它。 而且，每一句话对你都有用。 额外奖励：向以德语为母语的人询问您的 Anki 甲板句子。 他们可能会很乐意与您谈论德语，因为您会表现出您的承诺。

children.author: bryanrasmussen
children.textRawHtml:
>everyone under the age of 50 speaks good English and switched to that as soon as my first badly-conjugated sentence left my mouth.

OK maybe things have changed in recent years. I was always annoyed when coming to visit family living in Berlin for a couple days or driving in to a conference, and inevitably there would come a time when I would have to ask someone for help and I would have to ask in English because I do not speak German (although due to youthful immersion I start to remember it again after a couple of weeks) and they would inevitably answer in German.

I could somewhat understand when we stopped in a park asking where the convention center was of just a couple people by the side of the road, but asking a ticket agent at a train station what route I should take to get to a particular station and getting replies in German seemed especially rude.

At any rate despite some good experiences with Germans regarding helpfulness I have had a few too many negative experiences.
children.textZhHtml:
>每个 50 岁以下的人都会说一口流利的英语，当我说出第一句话时，我就开始说英语了。

好吧，也许近年来情况已经发生了变化。当我来柏林探望几天的家人或开车去参加会议时，我总是很恼火，不可避免地会有这样的时候，我必须向某人寻求帮助，我必须用英语询问，因为我不会说德语（尽管由于年轻的沉浸感，几周后我又开始记住它），他们不可避免地会用德语回答。

当我们在公园停下来询问会议中心在哪里时，旁边只有几个人，我可以理解。路，但在火车站询问售票员我应该走哪条路线才能到达特定车站并得到德语答复似乎特别粗鲁。

无论如何，尽管德国人在帮助方面有一些良好的经验，但我还是有过太多负面的经历。

children.author: marcus_holmes
children.textRawHtml:
Berliners are renowned even in Germany for being blunt to the point of rudeness.

What we in the Anglosphere would consider rude, they consider efficient. It can be very off-putting at first, but you do get used to it.
children.textZhHtml:
即使在德国，柏林人也因直率到粗鲁而闻名。

我们在英语圈中认为粗鲁的事情，在他们看来却是高效的。一开始可能会非常令人不快，但你确实会习惯它。

children.author: bryanrasmussen
children.textRawHtml: in most other countries renowned for being efficient it would not be considered efficient to answer in a language the questioner evidently does not speak.
children.textZhHtml: 在大多数其他以高效着称的国家，用提问者显然不会说的语言回答不会被认为是高效的。

children.author: aveao
children.textRawHtml:
This isn't really true. I've lived in both Munich and Hamburg now, and you 100% need some amount of german to navigate life, many people do not in fact have conversational-level english. I often say that the common paths tend to be easy to navigate in english (e.g. booking a train ticket, getting a phone line), but the adjacent flows (e.g. filing a refund request for your unfulfilled seat reservation, cancelling your phone contract) often require talking to someone in german. A doctor may be marked as speaking english, but that doesn't mean that their reception speaks english.

Berlin may be a different story within Germany, and then there's the entirety of e.g. Netherlands, but I know that the situation in France isn't better than here.

---

That said, B1 is a difficult level within german. Being able to converse fluently enough in not-quite-correct german -which is enough for getting through day to day life- is not necessarily enough to pass the test, and you really need to crunch through memorizing the grammatical gender of thousands of words, alongside getting really good at applying them in various grammatical forms (e.g. declensions vary for the same word based on how it's used).
children.textZhHtml:
这不是真的。我现在住在慕尼黑和汉堡，你100%需要一定程度的德语来驾驭生活，很多人实际上没有会话水平的英语。我经常说，常见的路径往往很容易用英语导航（例如预订火车票、拨打电话线），但相邻的流程（例如为未完成的座位预订提出退款请求、取消电话合同）通常需要与用德语的人交谈。医生可能被标记为说英语，但这并不意味着他们的接待处说英语。

在德国柏林可能是一个不同的故事，然后还有整个例如。荷兰，但我知道法国的情况并不比这里好。

---

话虽如此，B1在德语中是一个困难的级别。能够用不完全正确的德语进行足够流利的交谈（这足以应付日常生活）并不一定足以通过测试，你确实需要记住数千个单词的语法性别，同时真正擅长以各种语法形式应用它们（例如，同一个单词的词尾变化根据其使用方式而有所不同）。

children.author: stnikolauswagne
children.textRawHtml:
There is some sort of weird phenomenon that I cant quite put to words, but the core is:

While there is a big chunk of the native german population speaking great english there is a subset of (often) service workers who... dont. Had multiple furniture movers come through my house the last few weeks, the only way to communicate with them was broken german, since they had no english skills and my (croatian? serbian?) skills were zero.

At one point I ended up in the weirdest situation of my live were i translated the german instructions from the phone rep into english to the shift lead of the moving company so he could then give them in the native language to the movers.
children.textZhHtml:
有一种奇怪的现象，我无法用语言来形容，但其核心是：

虽然有很大一部分德国本土人口能说流利的英语，但有一小部分（通常）服务人员......却不会。过去几周，有多名家具搬运工来到我家，与他们沟通的唯一方式是蹩脚的德语，因为他们没有英语技能，而我的（克罗地亚语？塞尔维亚语？）技能为零。

有一次，我陷入了一生中最奇怪的境地，我将电话代表的德语指令翻译成英语，交给搬家公司的值班领导，这样他就可以用母语将它们交给搬运工。

children.author: bulbar
children.textRawHtml:
That's basically why you should learn the language of the country you live in.

Blaming people for not speaking English in Germany is pretty weird. How about learning German instead?
children.textZhHtml:
这就是为什么你应该学习你所居住国家的语言的原因。

在德国指责人们不会说英语是很奇怪的。学习德语怎么样？

children.author: stnikolauswagne
children.textRawHtml: I speak german as a mothertongue and fluent english, and I had massive troubles communicating in my own home.
children.textZhHtml: 我的母语是德语，英语也很流利，但我在自己家里沟通时遇到了很大的困难。
